title: Time Series Analysis & Forecasting
description: Foundations of time series analysis: stationarity, decomposition, ARIMA, exponential smoothing, Prophet, deep learning for TS, and walk-forward validation.
details: Time series data is everywhere — demand forecasting, anomaly detection, financial modeling, capacity planning. This file covers the complete toolkit: stationarity and differencing, autocorrelation and partial autocorrelation analysis, trend-seasonality decomposition, ARIMA/SARIMA models, exponential smoothing (Holt-Winters), Facebook Prophet, LSTMs and Transformers for time series, walk-forward cross-validation, evaluation metrics (MASE, sMAPE), and change point detection. Understanding time series is essential for roles involving forecasting, monitoring, and operations at FAANG.

sections.title: Stationarity & Differencing
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: A stationary time series has constant mean, variance, and autocorrelation over time. Most models assume stationarity. Non-stationary data must be transformed — usually by differencing.

Differencing: Replace each value with the difference from the previous: y't = yt - yt-1. First difference removes linear trend. Second difference removes quadratic trend. Seasonal differencing: y't = yt - yt-m where m = season period.

Statistical tests:

  • ADF Test (Augmented Dickey-Fuller): Null hypothesis: series has a unit root (non-stationary). p < 0.05 → reject H₀ → stationary. High p-value → need differencing.
  • KPSS Test: Null hypothesis: series IS stationary. p < 0.05 → reject H₀ → non-stationary. Use both ADF and KPSS — ADF fails to reject trends, KPSS fails to reject level shifts.

[Concrete Numbers] Concrete: Daily website traffic. ADF p = 0.32 (non-stationary). After first difference: ADF p = 0.001 (stationary). Model on differenced data, then reverse the difference on predictions.
sections.codeBlock:
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from statsmodels.tsa.stattools import adfuller, kpss
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# === ADF Test ===
def check_stationarity(series, title=''):
    result = adfuller(series.dropna(), autolag='AIC')
    print(f'{title} ADF: p={result[1]:.4f} ({"stationary" if result[1] < 0.05 else "non-stationary"})')
    return result[1] < 0.05

# === KPSS Test (complementary) ===
def check_kpss(series):
    result = kpss(series.dropna(), regression='c', nlags='auto')
    print(f'KPSS: p={result[1]:.4f} ({"non-stationary" if result[1] < 0.05 else "stationary"})')

# === Differencing ===
df['sales_diff_1'] = df['sales'].diff(1)      # first difference
df['sales_diff_7'] = df['sales'].diff(7)      # weekly seasonal difference
df['sales_log'] = np.log1p(df['sales'])        # log transform for variance stabilization

# Check if differencing made it stationary
check_stationarity(df['sales'], 'Original')
check_stationarity(df['sales_diff_1'].dropna(), 'First difference')

# === Reverse differencing for predictions ===
def inverse_diff(original, predictions, lag=1):
    return original.iloc[-lag:].values + np.cumsum(predictions)

sections.title: ACF, PACF & Model Identification
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Autocorrelation shows how a series relates to its past values. ACF and PACF plots are the “fingerprints” that tell you which ARIMA model to use.

Key concepts:

  • ACF (Autocorrelation Function): Correlation between yt and yt-k for different lags k. Includes indirect effects (yt correlated with yt-2 because both are correlated with yt-1).
  • PACF (Partial Autocorrelation Function): Correlation between yt and yt-k after removing the effect of intermediate lags (yt-1, ..., yt-k+1). Shows the direct contribution of each lag.

Reading the plots (Box-Jenkins methodology):

  • AR(p): ACF decays gradually (exponential or sinusoidal). PACF cuts off after lag p (sharp drop to near-zero). p = number of significant PACF lags.
  • MA(q): ACF cuts off after lag q. PACF decays gradually. q = number of significant ACF lags.
  • ARMA(p,q): Both ACF and PACF decay gradually — no clear cutoff. Use information criteria (AIC/BIC) to select p,q.
  • Seasonal ARIMA: ACF shows significant spikes at seasonal lags (e.g., lag 7, 14, 21 for weekly data). PACF shows a spike at the seasonal period. Add seasonal terms (P,D,Q,m).

[Practical Workflow] Pipeline: (1) Make series stationary (differencing). (2) Plot ACF and PACF of differenced series. (3) Identify candidate p,q from cutoff patterns. (4) Fit ARIMA(p,d,q) and compare AIC. (5) Check residuals — should be white noise (no significant autocorrelation).
sections.codeBlock:
from statsmodels.graphics.tsaplots import plot_acf, plot_pacf
from statsmodels.tsa.arima.model import ARIMA
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# === Plot ACF and PACF ===
fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(10, 6))
plot_acf(df['sales_diff_1'].dropna(), lags=40, ax=ax1)
plot_pacf(df['sales_diff_1'].dropna(), lags=40, ax=ax2, method='ywm')
plt.show()

# === Automated order selection (AIC) ===
from statsmodels.tsa.arima.model import ARIMA
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

best_aic = float('inf')
best_order = None

for p in range(0, 5):
    for q in range(0, 5):
        try:
            model = ARIMA(series, order=(p, 1, q))
            result = model.fit()
            if result.aic < best_aic:
                best_aic = result.aic
                best_order = (p, 1, q)
        except:
            continue

print(f'Best ARIMA order: {best_order}, AIC: {best_aic:.2f}')

# === Residual diagnostics ===
model = ARIMA(series, order=best_order)
result = model.fit()
residuals = result.resid

# Check residuals are white noise
from statsmodels.stats.diagnostic import acorr_ljungbox
lb_test = acorr_ljungbox(residuals, lags=[10, 20, 30], return_df=True)
print(lb_test)
# p > 0.05 = residuals are white noise (good fit!)

sections.title: ARIMA & SARIMA Models
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: ARIMA combines three components: AutoRegression (uses past values), Integrated (differencing to make stationary), and Moving Average (uses past forecast errors). Like a recipe: AR handles the momentum, I handles the trend, MA handles the noise.

ARIMA(p,d,q) components:

  • AR(p): yt = c + φ₁yt-1 + φ₂yt-2 + ... + φpyt-p + εt. Uses p past observations as predictors. Captures persistence/momentum.
  • I(d): Number of differences to achieve stationarity. d=1 for linear trend, d=2 for quadratic trend. Never use d > 2.
  • MA(q): yt = c + εt + θ₁εt-1 + ... + θqεt-q. Uses q past forecast errors. Captures shock effects that persist for q periods.

SARMIA(p,d,q)(P,D,Q)m: Adds seasonal terms. P = seasonal AR, D = seasonal differencing, Q = seasonal MA, m = number of periods per season. Example: weekly data with yearly seasonality → m=52. Daily data with weekly seasonality → m=7.

Key diagnostics:

  • AIC/BIC: Lower is better. BIC penalizes complexity more (prefers simpler models).
  • Residuals: Should be white noise (no autocorrelation). If Ljung-Box p < 0.05, the model missed some structure — increase p,q or add seasonality.
  • Forecast intervals: ARIMA gives prediction intervals that widen with forecast horizon. Wider intervals = more uncertainty.
sections.codeBlock:
from statsmodels.tsa.arima.model import ARIMA
from statsmodels.tsa.statespace.sarimax import SARIMAX
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# === ARIMA ===
# p=2 (use 2 past values), d=1 (first diff), q=2 (use 2 past errors)
model = ARIMA(series, order=(2, 1, 2))
result = model.fit()
print(result.summary())

# Forecast next 10 steps
forecast = result.forecast(steps=10)
# Get prediction intervals
pred = result.get_forecast(steps=10)
pred_ci = pred.conf_int(alpha=0.05)  # 95% CI

# === SARIMA (with weekly seasonality) ===
model = SARIMAX(
    series,
    order=(1, 1, 1),          # non-seasonal (p,d,q)
    seasonal_order=(1, 1, 1, 7),  # seasonal (P,D,Q,m) — weekly
    enforce_stationarity=False,
    enforce_invertibility=False
)
result = model.fit(disp=False)
print(f'SARIMA AIC: {result.aic:.2f}')

# === Auto ARIMA (auto-select orders) ===
# pmdarima library (pip install pmdarima)
# from pmdarima import auto_arima
# model = auto_arima(series, seasonal=True, m=7,
#                     stepwise=True, trace=True)
# print(model.summary())

# === Walk-forward cross-validation for ARIMA ===
def walk_forward_cv(series, model_class, order, h=1, n_windows=5):
    errors = []
    for i in range(n_windows):
        train = series[:-(n_windows - i) - h]
        test = series[-(n_windows - i) - h:-(n_windows - i)]
        
        model = model_class(train, order=order)
        result = model.fit()
        pred = result.forecast(steps=h)
        errors.append(np.mean((pred.values - test.values) ** 2))
    return np.mean(errors)

sections.title: Exponential Smoothing (Holt-Winters)
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Exponential smoothing gives more weight to recent observations, with weights decaying exponentially. Holt-Winters extends this to capture both trend and seasonality.

Three variants:

  • Simple Exponential Smoothing (SES): ŷt+1 = αyt + (1-α)ŷt. Only level (no trend, no seasonality). α near 1 = trust recent observations. α near 0 = trust historical average.
  • Holt's Linear Trend: Adds trend component: levelt = αyt + (1-α)(levelt-1 + trendt-1); trendt = β(levelt - levelt-1) + (1-β)trendt-1. Forecast: levelt + h · trendt.
  • Holt-Winters (Triple): Adds seasonal component. Additive seasonality (magnitude constant) vs multiplicative (magnitude grows with level). Forecast: (level + h · trend) × seasont+h-m for multiplicative.

Parameter selection:

  • α (level): 0 to 1. High α = model adapts quickly to changes. Low α = smooth, slow to react.
  • β (trend): 0 to 1. High β = trend updates quickly. Set β=0 if trend is stable.
  • γ (seasonality): 0 to 1. High γ = seasonal pattern updates quickly. Set γ=0 if seasonality is stable.
  • φ (damping): Dampens trend to flat line in long-term forecasts. φ near 1 = minimal damping. φ near 0 = trend dies quickly. Damped trends are safer for long horizons.
sections.codeBlock:
from statsmodels.tsa.holtwinters import (
    SimpleExpSmoothing, 
    Holt, 
    ExponentialSmoothing
)
import numpy as np

# === Simple Exponential Smoothing ===
model = SimpleExpSmoothing(series)
result = model.fit(smoothing_level=0.3, optimized=False)
result = model.fit()  # auto-optimize alpha
print(f'Optimal alpha: {result.params["smoothing_level"]:.3f}')
forecast = result.forecast(10)

# === Holt's Linear Trend ===
model = Holt(series, damped_trend=True)
result = model.fit()
print(f'Alpha: {result.params["smoothing_level"]:.3f}')
print(f'Beta:  {result.params["smoothing_trend"]:.3f}')
print(f'Phi:   {result.params["damping_trend"]:.3f}')

# === Holt-Winters (with seasonality) ===
model = ExponentialSmoothing(
    series,
    seasonal_periods=7,      # weekly seasonality
    trend='add',
    seasonal='add',           # or 'mul' for multiplicative
    damped_trend=True
)
result = model.fit()
print(result.summary())

forecast = result.forecast(14)  # 2 weeks ahead

# === Compare models with AIC ===
models = {
    'SES': SimpleExpSmoothing(series).fit(),
    'Holt': Holt(series, damped_trend=True).fit(),
    'HW': ExponentialSmoothing(series, seasonal_periods=7,
                                trend='add', seasonal='add').fit()
}
for name, m in models.items():
    print(f'{name}: AIC={m.aic:.1f}')

# Lower AIC = better fit (penalizes complexity)

sections.title: Prophet (Facebook)
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Prophet decomposes time series into trend, seasonality, and holidays — but unlike ARIMA, it's designed for business forecasting with missing data, outliers, and human-interpretable parameters.

Prophet model: y(t) = g(t) + s(t) + h(t) + εt

  • g(t): Trend — Piecewise linear or logistic growth with changepoints. Automatically detects where the trend changes direction. Set changepoint_prior_scale to control flexibility (higher = more changepoints).
  • s(t): Seasonality — Fourier series approximation of periodic effects. Supports yearly, weekly, daily seasonality. Add custom seasonalities (monthly, hourly).
  • h(t): Holidays — List of holiday dates with windows (days before/after). Prophet learns the effect of each holiday. Critical for retail forecasting (Black Friday, Christmas).
  • εt: Unusual events (outliers) handled implicitly by the Bayesian framework.

Key strengths over ARIMA:

  • Handles missing data natively (gaps in time series are fine).
  • Robust to outliers (Bayesian prior prevents overfitting to extreme values).
  • Intuitive parameters with clear business meaning (changepoint_prior_scale, seasonality_prior_scale, holidays_prior_scale).
  • Forecast intervals via Monte Carlo simulation.
  • Built-in changepoint detection — finds when the trend direction changes automatically.
sections.codeBlock:
from prophet import Prophet
import pandas as pd

# === Prepare data (Prophet requires specific column names) ===
df = df.rename(columns={'date': 'ds', 'value': 'y'})

# === Basic Prophet ===
model = Prophet(
    yearly_seasonality=True,
    weekly_seasonality=True,
    daily_seasonality=False,
    changepoint_prior_scale=0.05,  # default — higher = more changepoints
    seasonality_prior_scale=10.0,   # default — higher = stronger seasonality
    holidays_prior_scale=10.0,      # default
    changepoint_range=0.8           # only search for changepoints in first 80%
)

# Add holidays
model.add_country_holidays(country_name='US')

# Add custom seasonality (monthly)
model.add_seasonality(name='monthly', period=30.5, fourier_order=5)

model.fit(df)

# === Forecast ===
future = model.make_future_dataframe(periods=90)  # 90 days ahead
forecast = model.predict(future)
print(forecast[['ds', 'yhat', 'yhat_lower', 'yhat_upper']].tail())

# === Plot components ===
fig = model.plot(forecast)
fig2 = model.plot_components(forecast)

# === Changepoint detection ===
from prophet.plot import add_changepoints_to_plot
fig = model.plot(forecast)
a = add_changepoints_to_plot(fig.gca(), model, forecast)

# === Cross-validation ===
from prophet.diagnostics import cross_validation, performance_metrics

df_cv = cross_validation(
    model, initial='730 days', period='180 days', horizon='365 days'
)
df_p = performance_metrics(df_cv)
print(df_p.head())

sections.title: Deep Learning for Time Series (LSTM, Transformer)
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Deep learning shines when you have lots of data, multiple related time series, or complex nonlinear patterns that classical methods can't capture.

When to use DL for TS:

  • ARIMA/Prophet fail (complex patterns, multiple seasonalities)
  • Multi-step forecasts with many related series (demand for 1000s of products)
  • You have exogenous features (price, promotions, weather) that impact the target
  • Large datasets (100K+ time steps)

LSTM for time series: Use a sliding window of past values (e.g., 30 days) to predict the next day. Stacked LSTMs with dropout for regularization. Output can be single-step or multi-step (seq2seq with decoder).

Transformer for time series: Informer, Autoformer, and PatchTST are state-of-the-art. The key innovation is treating time series “patches” (subsequences) as tokens, processing them with self-attention. Transformers capture long-range dependencies better than LSTMs.

Key considerations:

  • Scaling: Always scale time series to [0,1] or standardize. Use the same scaler for all series in multi-series problems.
  • Teacher forcing: For multi-step forecasting, use scheduled sampling or autoregressive decoding with ground truth during training.
  • Loss function: MSE works well. For probabilistic forecasts, use negative log-likelihood or quantile loss (pinball loss).
sections.codeBlock:
import numpy as np
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

# === Prepare sequences for LSTM ===
def create_sequences(data, seq_length=30, pred_length=1):
    X, y = [], []
    for i in range(len(data) - seq_length - pred_length + 1):
        X.append(data[i:i+seq_length])
        y.append(data[i+seq_length:i+seq_length+pred_length])
    return np.array(X), np.array(y)

# === Simple LSTM model ===
class LSTMForecaster(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, input_dim=1, hidden_dim=64, num_layers=2, output_dim=1):
        super().__init__()
        self.lstm = nn.LSTM(input_dim, hidden_dim, num_layers, 
                            batch_first=True, dropout=0.2)
        self.fc = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, output_dim)
    
    def forward(self, x):
        out, _ = self.lstm(x)  # out: (batch, seq, hidden)
        out = self.fc(out[:, -1, :])  # take last time step
        return out

# === Training loop (simplified) ===
# model = LSTMForecaster()
# optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3)
# criterion = nn.MSELoss()
#
# for epoch in range(100):
#     for batch_X, batch_y in loader:
#         optimizer.zero_grad()
#         pred = model(batch_X)
#         loss = criterion(pred, batch_y)
#         loss.backward()
#         optimizer.step()

# === Features for DL time series ===
# 1. Lag features: values at t-1, t-2, ..., t-30
# 2. Rolling statistics: mean, std, min, max over windows
# 3. Time features: hour, day, month, dayofweek, sin/cos encoding
# 4. Exogenous: price, promo, weather, holidays
# 5. Series ID embedding (for multi-series models)

# === Multi-series architecture ===
# Shared LSTM across all series, with learned series embeddings
# class MultiSeriesLSTM(nn.Module):
#     def __init__(self, n_series, embed_dim=8, ...):
#         self.series_embedding = nn.Embedding(n_series, embed_dim)
#         self.lstm = nn.LSTM(input_dim + embed_dim, ...)

# === Evaluation metrics for TS ===
# from sklearn.metrics import mean_absolute_percentage_error
# mape = np.mean(np.abs((y_true - y_pred) / y_true)) * 100
# 
# def smape(y_true, y_pred):
#     return 100 * np.mean(2 * np.abs(y_true - y_pred) / 
#                          (np.abs(y_true) + np.abs(y_pred)))

sections.title: Walk-Forward Validation & Evaluation
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Time series data has temporal order — random shuffling destroys the autocorrelation structure. Walk-forward validation respects the temporal ordering: train on past, predict on future, and never the reverse.

Validation strategies:

  • Walk-Forward (Expanding Window): Start with a minimum training size, predict next h steps. Expand training to include those steps, predict next h. Repeat until data ends. Most thorough — uses all data for both training and evaluation.
  • Sliding Window: Fixed training window size. Slide window forward, predict next h steps. Better for non-stationary series (old data may be irrelevant). Less training data per fold.
  • Time Series Split (sklearn): Pre-defined split points based on time. E.g., train on 2018-2019, validate on 2020. Simple and interpretable.

Time series evaluation metrics:

  • MASE (Mean Absolute Scaled Error): MAE / MAEnaive. The error relative to a naive forecast (predict previous value). Values < 1 mean your model beats the naive baseline. Scale-independent, so comparable across series.
  • sMAPE (Symmetric MAPE): 200/n · Σ|yt - ŷt| / (|yt| + |ŷt|). Avoids MAPE's problem of division by zero and asymmetry.
  • Forecast bias: Σ(ŷt - yt) / Σyt. Positive = over-forecasting, negative = under-forecasting. Should be near 0.
sections.codeBlock:
from sklearn.model_selection import TimeSeriesSplit
import numpy as np

# === TimeSeriesSplit (sklearn) ===
tscv = TimeSeriesSplit(n_splits=5, test_size=30)  # 5 folds, 30 days test each

for fold, (train_idx, test_idx) in enumerate(tscv.split(X)):
    X_train, X_test = X[train_idx], X[test_idx]
    y_train, y_test = y[train_idx], y[test_idx]
    
    model = ARIMA(y_train, order=(1, 1, 1))
    result = model.fit()
    pred = result.forecast(steps=len(y_test))
    
    mase = np.mean(np.abs(y_test - pred)) / np.mean(np.abs(np.diff(y_train)))
    print(f'Fold {fold}: MASE={mase:.3f}')

# === Custom walk-forward ===
def walk_forward_validation(series, model_fn, h=1, min_train=100):
    """
    model_fn: callable that takes (train_series) and returns forecast function
    """
    n = len(series)
    predictions = []
    actuals = []
    
    for t in range(min_train, n - h + 1):
        train = series[:t]
        actual = series[t:t+h]
        
        forecast = model_fn(train, h)
        predictions.append(forecast)
        actuals.append(actual)
    
    return np.array(predictions), np.array(actuals)

# === Evaluation functions ===
def smape(y_true, y_pred):
    return 100 * np.mean(2 * np.abs(y_true - y_pred) / 
                         (np.abs(y_true) + np.abs(y_pred) + 1e-8))

def mase(y_true, y_pred, y_train):
    naive_error = np.mean(np.abs(np.diff(y_train)))
    return np.mean(np.abs(y_true - y_pred)) / naive_error

# === Naive baseline (always beats?) ===
# If your model doesn't beat the naive forecast, it's useless
naive_pred = series[-h-1:-1]  # predict previous value
print(f'Naive MASE: {mase(actuals, naive_pred, train):.3f}')
